text: Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Marine and Petroleum Geology journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/marpetgeo Research paper Mass transport deposit (MTD) relief as a control on post-MTD sedimentation: Insights from the Taranaki Basin, oﬀshore New Zealand Jeﬀerson Nwoko∗, Ian Kane, Mads Huuse Basin Studies, School of Natural Sciences, University of Manchester, UK A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Mass transport deposits (MTDs) Turbidite Stratigraphic trap Seismic facies A B S T R A C T Mass transport deposits (MTDs) can aﬀect the routing of turbidite systems and their resultant sedimentary ar- chitecture. With the aid of a high-quality 3D-seismic reﬂection dataset a buried Pleistocene MTD has been identiﬁed in the deep-water Taranaki Basin. Three MTD seismic facies have been identiﬁed and fall within three morphological domains – extensional, translational and contractional areas. Thickness relationships between the MTD and the overburden deposits were investigated quantitatively and matched using TWT-thickness maps. The results reveal that in proximal and distal areas with signiﬁcant MTD relief, occupied by megaclasts and folded facies respectively, post-MTD accommodation is dominated by small ‘micro-basins’ with tortuous sediment pathways. In contrast the translational domain, containing homogeneous MTD facies, hosts larger low-relief basins. These characteristics can be used to deﬁne two main types of depositional topography: Type I, High-relief depositional topography, is observed prominently in the headwall and toe regions of MTDs, resulting in loosely- connected and isolated ponds of sediment; Type II, Low-relief depositional topography, is observed in the trans- lational domain where the eﬀects of sediment loading are clearly observed. Turbidite sandstone distribution aﬀected by Type I topography, is characterised by irregular pinch-outs which present a signiﬁcant risk for stratigraphic trapping whereas Type II topography may form overlying sedimentary bodies which are more extensive. Turbidite deposits in both settings are prone to syn-depositional remobilisation on

Turbidite deposits in both settings are prone to syn-depositional remobilisation on top of the MTD, which may introduce considerable heterogeneity, particularly at reservoir margins. 1. Introduction Mass Transport Deposits (MTDs) are sedimentary bodies that are translated downslope from topographic highs and slopes to areas of lower gradient as a result of gravity–induced mass failure (Hampton et al., 1996; Moscardelli and Wood, 2008; Kneller et al., 2016; Nwoko et al., 2020). They commonly contain sediments remobilised from re- latively shallower water depth which have been redeposited on the slopes and ﬂoor of deep water basins (Posamentier and Walker, 2006; Kneller et al., 2016). MTDs may cover large areas (kilometres across) and are therefore very important to exploration and production activ- ities in many hydrocarbon provinces; acting as seals or preconditioning reservoir geometries (Armitage et al., 2009; Omeru, 2014). The oﬀ- shore Taranaki Basin (New Zealand) includes world-class examples of MTDs both in seismic sections from the oﬀshore subsurface and exposed along the coast (Strachan, 2002; King et al., 2011). From their initiation point through to their termination point, MTDs create diﬀerent accommodation proﬁles through a combination of erosion and deposition (Olaﬁranye et al., 2013). By deﬁnition, accommodation is the space created above the sediment surface – the background slope surface, but below the equilibrium proﬁle, i.e.an ideal slope proﬁle where no erosion and/or deposition takes place (Pirmez et al., 2000; Kneller, 2003).

From their initiation point through to their termination point, MTDs create diﬀerent accommodation proﬁles through a combination of erosion and deposition (Olaﬁranye et al., 2013). By deﬁnition, accommodation is the space created above the sediment surface – the background slope surface, but below the equilibrium proﬁle, i.e.an ideal slope proﬁle where no erosion and/or deposition takes place (Pirmez et al., 2000; Kneller, 2003). MTDs aﬀect seaﬂoor morphology, post-MTD strata deposition and deformation, and can control submarine channel and lobe location (Von Huene et al., 2004; Hühnerbach and Masson, 2004; Hühnerbach et al., 2005). The internal characteristics of MTDs provide insight into how intrinsic structural features such as folding, stratal disaggregation and diﬀerential compaction can create topographic highs and lows which will aﬀect later turbidite deposition (e.g. Kneller et al., 2016). Com- plicated onlap or downlap relationships may develop against these depositional highs depending on their orientation. The inﬂuence that MTD-related bathymetric lows and highs have depends on the scale of the accommodation space with respect to incoming turbidity currents; end-members may be (i) isolated ponded reservoirs, or (ii) tortuously connected reservoirs (Kneller et al., 2016). Exposed outcrop examples of sedimentary units deposited around and on top of MTDs have been recognised and illustrate their interac- tion from bed scale to reservoir scale. However, due to a lack of high resolution 3D seismic-reﬂection data explicitly resolving the eﬀect of MTD surface topography on post-depositional strata, post-MTD sedi- ment connectivity in the subsurface is poorly understood (e.g. Pickering and Corregidor, 2000; Armitage et al., 2009; Jackson and Johnson, 2009). This paper examines how internal architecture and external morphology of MTDs are related and how the latter controls subsequent turbidite deposition. We do this by studying an exceptionally well-im- aged MTD in a 3D seismic volume from oﬀshore Taranaki, New Zealand. We investigate this by: a) Characterising and interpreting the seismic facies associated with the MTD and how diﬀerent MTD domains aﬀected depositional re- lief; b) Studying the internal architecture of the MTD, kinematic indicators and basal structures associated with the deposit in order to establish their impact on topography creation throughout the developmental stage, from the initiation point to their termination; c) Analysing how diﬀerent accommodation styles aﬀect the amount of sediment accumulation and the type of facies developed above. 2. Tectonic and stratigraphic setting The study area covers an area of ca. 1925 km2 of the deep-water Taranaki Basin, which has a total area of ca. 330,000 km2 (Panpichityota et al., 2018) (Fig. 1a and b). Taranaki Basin initially evolved as a marine basin, as part of the new Caledonian Basin (now located north-west of the present day Taranaki Basin), and was created by the interaction of the Australian and Paciﬁc convergent plates (Uruski, 2010). Structurally, Taranaki Basin evolved from a passive to active margin basin through time. Starting in the mid Cretaceous, spreading of the Tasman Sea and the break-up of Eastern Gondwana resulted in localised fault-controlled extensional subsidence leading to the development of rifted sub-basins now considered to be a failed continental rift (Laird, 1981, 1994). The Taranaki rift formed the associated northern west coast rift connected to the southern part of the Tasman Sea ridge and interpreted to be part of the rift transform system (Thrasher, 1990; King and Thrasher, 1992). Basin-wide subsidence and post-rift thermal contraction characterised the basin history from the late Eocene to early Oligocene. Throughout the Neogene, the oﬀshore deep-water part of the Taranaki Basin developed on a gently subsiding passive margin, and experienced a complicated tectonic history with periods of active margin extension, rapid deepening, and compression, occurring si- multaneously in diﬀerent areas of the basin (King and Thrasher, 1992; Giba et al., 2010). The depositional history of the Taranaki Basin is recorded by up to 8 km of sediment deposited during a transgressive – regressive cycle from the Late Cretaceous to Early Miocene

the Neogene, the oﬀshore deep-water part of the Taranaki Basin developed on a gently subsiding passive margin, and experienced a complicated tectonic history with periods of active margin extension, rapid deepening, and compression, occurring si- multaneously in diﬀerent areas of the basin (King and Thrasher, 1992; Giba et al., 2010). The depositional history of the Taranaki Basin is recorded by up to 8 km of sediment deposited during a transgressive – regressive cycle from the Late Cretaceous to Early Miocene (Fig. 1c). The early Oligo- cene marked the cessation of sediment supply, where transgression progressively shifted sedimentation southwards (King and Thrasher, 1996). This depositional hiatus in the southern Taranaki region – known as the “base Oligocene paraconformity”, separates the passive margin phase from the active margin phase (King and Thrasher, 1992); while Miocene sedimentation was inﬂuenced by the evolving Australian - Paciﬁc plate transform boundary (the Alpine fault). The middle Mio- cene saw the end of compression within the northern part of the basin and at the same time, the Mohakatino submarine volcanic arc formation began (King and Thrasher, 1992; 1996). These volcanic mounds con- trolled the distribution of sediments with low amplitude, chaotic re- ﬂections identiﬁed and interpreted as MTDs interbedded with channels and turbidites up until the late Pliocene (King and Thrasher, 1996; Uruski, 2010). These sedimentary units occur below base of prograda- tional clinoforms, identiﬁed as the Giant Foresets Formation (Salazar et al., 2016). During the late Pleistocene, several feeder canyons cut into the shelf, intercepting along-shore and ﬂuvial sediment supply and diverting it to deeper parts of the basin. These canyons feed the nu- merous basin ﬂoor submarine channel systems ubiquitous on the pre- sent day seaﬂoor – Fig. 1b (King and Thrasher, 1992). These canyons feed the nu- merous basin ﬂoor submarine channel systems ubiquitous on the pre- sent day seaﬂoor – Fig. 1b (King and Thrasher, 1992). A chronostratigraphic framework for the basin has been developed using biostratigraphic data from the nearby oﬀshore well (Romney-1, Fig. 1d) which constrains the age of the youngest MTD (the MTD of study) to the Pleistocene (Rad, 2015; Nwoko et al., 2020). This MTD covers an estimated area of 22,397 km2, with a remobilised volume of ~3700 km3 (Omeru et al., 2016) (Fig. 1d). 3. Data and methods The dataset consists of a 3D seismic reﬂection survey covering an area of approximately 1925 km2 in the north western part of the deep- water Taranaki Basin, oﬀshore New Zealand (Fig. 1a and b). The Romney 3D seismic reﬂection data only images parts of the study MTD in the deep-water Taranaki Basin (Fig. 1b). This 3D seismic volume was acquired by a single sleeve Airgun source array and a 6 × 3500 m streamer array, sampled at an interval of 4 ms, and a dominant fre- quency of 41 Hz, resulting in a vertical resolution of about 9 m, with a sediment velocity of 1850 ms−1 (Rusconi, 2017; Nwoko et al., 2020). Bin spacing of the seismic volume is 12.5 m × 25 m. The bin spacing is such that the extent and stratigraphy of MTD is easily mappable. Data processing includes post-stack time migration (PSTM), resampling, automatic gain control (AGC) and zero phase conversion. Well data from the Romney-1 well comprise a full well

The bin spacing is such that the extent and stratigraphy of MTD is easily mappable. Data processing includes post-stack time migration (PSTM), resampling, automatic gain control (AGC) and zero phase conversion. Well data from the Romney-1 well comprise a full well log suite for a deeper exploration drilling objective and is available together with checkshot data that constrain the age of the MTD to be well above the top Miocene reﬂector where there are no log data available (Fig. 1c, d and 1e) (Nwoko et al., 2020). Methods for the morphometric characterisation of MTDs described by Clare et al. (2019) are used in this study. Mapping of the Pleistocene MTD required a combination of guided and autotracked seismic inter- pretation, interpreting the data at a constant grid spacing (16 × 16 m), given the chaotic nature of many of the internal reﬂections and the spatially variable nature of the bounding horizons. Negative amplitude reﬂections (denoted by blue troughs) indicate a decrease in acoustic impedance, while positive amplitudes (denoted by red peaks) on seismic reﬂection proﬁles represents an increase in acoustic impedance following the SEG-Normal polarity conventions. Internal seismic facies were deﬁned on selected seismic lines and have been imaged using several seismic attributes and variance slices from the 3D volume to generate amplitude, dip, thickness and RMS maps. These attributes il- luminated the abrupt truncations of the seismic reﬂections and de- formation styles within the MTD. TWT-thickness of overburden deposits (using the seaﬂoor as a reference horizon) has been generated to trace the development of post- MTD sedimentation inﬂuenced by MTD relief (Fig. 2). 4. Results 4.1. Basal shear surface Description: In the study area, the basal surface of the MTD is generally sub-parallel to the underlying undeformed stratigraphy (Fig. 3). In planform view, there is an extensive east-west trending feature that creates a step on the basal surface (Fig. 3a, b and 3c). This step-like geometry corresponds to a change in dip with a general NW dip – between 9° and 15° (Fig. 3a, b and 3c). The basal surface of this MTD coalesces with infrajacent stratigraphy making it diﬃcult to trace. J. Nwoko, et al. At the eastern part of the extensive E-W feature, are two prominent U-shaped depressions (Fig. 3b and e). On both sides of the depressions are N-S localised faults cutting through the underlying stratigraphy, detached at the same stratigraphic level as the U-shaped depressions (Fig. 3e). Three sets of elongate negative-relief linear fea- tures, 2–8 km long and 0.2–0.3 km wide, are identiﬁed on the basal surface of the MTD; these sets are labelled as L1, L2 and L3 (Fig. 3b). L1 linear features have a general NE-SW orientation sub-parallel to the E- W extensive feature, L2 and L3 have a SE – NW orientation but L3 are less well-imaged. Interpretation: Ramps step through stratigraphy whilst the ﬂats are parallel to stratigraphy (Bull et al., 2009). Ramps are useful palaeo- transport kinematic indicators and result from a slide stepping up through stratigraphic level(s) resulting in a step-like geometry (Frey- Martínez et al., 2006; Bull et al., 2009). Thus, the extensive E-W trending feature on the MTD basal surface is interpreted to be a basal ramp. The N-S faults that terminate beneath the basal surface de- marcate the blocks which appear to have undergone some degree of shearing towards the west (Omeru, 2014). Several authors suggest that lineations can also be palaeo-transport trend indicators and have interpreted these features to be grooves, formed by the erosional action of coherent blocks grinding into the substrate (Posamentier and Kolla, 2003; Bull et al., 2009; Nwoko et al., 2020). Hence a more eﬀective prediction of the overall MTD translation direction will combine lineation orienta- tions with the orientation of the ramp and the transport direction given by block organisation (Bull et al., 2009; Omeru and Cartwright, 2019). Ramps have also been reported to occur parallel to the direction of transport (Bull et al., 2009). The orientation of the identiﬁed basal ramp is laterally sub-parallel to the lineation orientations, thus we suggest a gross SW translation direction for this MTD. Additionally, since the N-S faults are localised to the eastern parts of the basal ramp only in the vicinity of the depressions, we interpret this basal ramp as a generally non-fault controlled lateral-frontal basal ramp.

The variability in orientation of L1 and L2 lineations could be attributed to rotation of blocks during translation (Alsop and Marco, 2014; Omeru and Cartwright, 2019); while the poorly imaged L3 lineations may be due to diﬀerences in substrate lithiﬁcation (Posamentier and Kolla, 2003; Nwoko et al., 2020). 4.2. MTD internal characteristics The general internal seismic expression of the MTD is chaotic in nature (Fig. 4). Three main seismic facies labelled 1) Megaclast facies (MF), 2) Homogeneous facies (HF), and 3) Folded facies (FF), can be observed from proximal northeast to distal southwest limits of the survey (Figs. 3a and 4a). This MTD has a tripartite morphology with an extensional domain – denoted by planar features, protruding mega- clasts on the seaﬂoor (Nwoko et al., 2020; their Fig. 3e) (Fig. 4b, c and 4d), a translational domain (Fig. 4a, e and 4f), and a contractional domain (arcuate scarps, thrusts features, frontal conﬁnement – Fig. 3b, d, 4a and 4f). The seismic facies associated with these MTD domains are described based on their seismic reﬂection attributes, amplitude re- ﬂection strength, internal conﬁguration, and continuity of the reﬂec- tions. The seismic facies associated with these MTD domains are described based on their seismic reﬂection attributes, amplitude re- ﬂection strength, internal conﬁguration, and continuity of the reﬂec- tions. MF description: This facies is well imaged and covers an area of approximately 413.82 km2. From northeast to southwest, a dominantly moderate to high amplitude reﬂection pattern that typically delineates angular protruding megaclasts is observed (Fig. 4b and c). The mega- clasts, measuring about 0.4–1.5 km high and 0.7 km–1 km in width, are internally stratiﬁed and characterised by coherent reﬂections that are separated by extensional planar contacts, encased within a chaotic mix (Fig. 4b and c). Within the same facies, a characteristic set of moder- ately deformed seismic reﬂections with some localised faults abruptly terminates against a series of relatively undeformed parallel reﬂectors (Fig. 4d). These undeformed parallel reﬂectors are mainly composed of symmetrical fore- and back-thrust sediment blocks (Fig. 4d). MF interpretation: This facies is interpreted to represent discrete megaclasts carried within a deformed (debrites) laminar ﬂow (Fig. 4b and c) (Frey-Martinez et al., 2005; Bull et al., 2009; Ortiz-Karpf et al., 2015; Omeru and Cartwright, 2019; Nwoko et al., 2020). Megaclasts within debris ﬂows have often been associated with disintegration of retrogressive failures into more chaotic packages in the downslope di- rection (Normandeau et al., 2019). Based on the conﬁguration, or- ientation, and the way they map out, we imply the megaclasts facies mainly occupy upstream, in the extensional domain, and that the megaclasts stopped while the material behind them was still in motion, building up and buckling behind the megaclasts. Thus, we interpret these fore-and back thrust structures to have formed during downslope translation of the MTD, and megaclast

HF description: This region of the MTD covers an area of ca. 197 km2 and is mostly restricted to the MTD translational region (Fig. 4a). Planform and cross-section imaging of this facies shows the conﬁguration to be dominated by homogenously disrupted and amor- phous low-amplitude reﬂection patterns (Fig. 4a, e and 4f). FF description: In the contractional domain, a package of thrusted and folded reﬂections (up to 200 ms TWT-thickness – Fig. 5a), sepa- rated by localised faults with folded planes that dip in a proximal di- rection, dominates the deposits just before the basal ramp (Fig. 4a and f). The deposit appears to extend beyond this frontal ramp further down-system where it overlies and coalesces with substrate strati- graphy (Fig. 3d). In planform, these folded reﬂections resembles fault traces that form a lateral margin against the frontal ramp and extends over a length of ca.42 km, covering an area of ca. 103.4 km2 (Fig. 3b). FF interpretation: Previous studies in analogous deposits have de- scribed these features as pressure ridges or imbricate thrusts associated with frontally-emergent MTDs (Posamentier and Kolla, 2003; Frey- Martínez et al., 2006; Moscardelli et al., 2006; Bull et al., 2009). Therefore, the dipping planes are interpreted as basin-ward directed syn-depositional thrust structures with alternating sequences of folds that detach from the basal surface (Fig. 4f and g). These were perhaps driven by the sliding MTD in the down-dip direction against and over the non-fault controlled basal ramp. 4.3. Top

Top MTD surface Description: The mapped surface shows a wide range of undulating topography from smooth to very rugose (Fig. 4). They often protrude from the seaﬂoor, creating complex surface rugosity in the extensional proximal parts (Fig. 4a and b and 4c). In the contractional distal regions of the survey, top MTD surface forms localised micro-basins for po- tential accumulation of thick-thin sequences of onlapped post-MTD sediments (Fig. 5b and c). Typical surface heights are between 100 and 350 m in the northeast dominated by megaclasts facies (Fig. 4b and c), as opposed to about 30–80 m in the contractional distal domain, dominated by the folded facies (Fig. 4f). Interpretation: A correlation between the character of the upper surface and the distribution of the MTD seismic facies can be estab- lished to provide insight into the development of the MTD topography and to estimate the amount of sediment ponding (Fig. 5c–g). The irre- gularity of the top surface in the extensional domain has been attrib- uted to the development of unburied megaclasts carried within the MTD, associated with retrogressive failure processes (Nwoko et al., 2020). Development of MTD depositional-type topography Slope stability is a function of the relationship between frictional and gravitational forces. Factors such as changes in slope loads, surface failure strength, slope gradient and nature of the basal shear surface can lead to instability (Sultan et al., 2004; Urgeles and Camerlenghi, 2013; Li et al., 2015). the MTD with their relief types aﬀect the amount of sediment distribution and connectivity. 5.1. Development of MTD depositional-type topography Slope stability is a function of the relationship between frictional and gravitational forces. Factors such as changes in slope loads, surface failure strength, slope gradient and nature of the basal shear surface can lead to instability (Sultan et al., 2004; Urgeles and Camerlenghi, 2013; Li et al., 2015). These factors can facilitate MTD development and emplacement. Based on the seismic character, deformation style and structural features of folds, stratal disaggregation and faults, two main types of MTD depositional-type topography are developed during the emplacement of the youngest MTD in the Taranaki area. They have been identiﬁed as Type I: High-relief depositional topography and Type II: Low-relief depositional topography, controlled by MTD seismic fa- cies (Fig. 7a, b and 7c). Therefore, MTD depositional relief types can be eﬀectively linked to the MTD seismic facies (Table 1). Though both MTD type 1 and II topography types are generated by depositional processes, the results of this study shows that high surface irregularity often represents an overlap between structural internal deformation and depositional events that produces the MTD seismic facies. Despite the wide range of deformation styles, and the topography which they developed, the common basal shear surface suggests that the MTD moved en-masse largely as a single event. The transition from type I to type II depositional topography typically occurs at the boundaries be- tween the facies (Fig. 4a, e, 4f and 7b). 5.1.1. Type I: high-relief depositional topography Locally developed structural conﬁnement is prevalent within the MF and FF and is responsible for the development of rugose upper surfaces, acting as barrier to connected sediment ponds. Towards the toe domain, topographic conﬁnement leads to freezing and abrupt deposition of the sediment mass,

Consequently, MTD type I topography not only accounts for this var- iation in relief but can be linked to particular MTD seismic facies. 5.1.2. Type II: low-relief depositional topography Low-relief depositional topography (type II) developed during em- placement is largely devoid of any structural imprint. Similar to pre- vious studies (Armitage et al., 2009; Olaﬁranye et al., 2013; Bull et al., 2020), this topography occurs within the translational domain of the MTD, characterised by smooth upper surfaces associated with the highly deformed amorphous nature of the HF facies (Figs. 4f, 6a and 7b). Additionally, since the greatest overburden sediment thickness is observed within this type of topography, it is inferred that MTD facies herein have undergone signiﬁcant disintegration to allow subsidence and lateral spreading of the MTD, thus leading to the accumulation of more connected post-MTD stratigraphy (Figs. 5b and 7b). This corre- sponds to lower regression (R2) values for this MTD-type topography as opposed to higher R2 values for MTD type I (Fig. 8a). 5.2. Modiﬁcation of sediment distribution and connectivity by MTD relief Accommodation space for the deposition of overburden deposits, especially on an MTD-controlled topography, can be inﬂuenced by rate of deformation, sediment supply and ﬂow power, and would be con- tinuously adjusted until the system reaches equilibrium (Kneller, 2003; Ferry et al., 2005). Thus, we have identiﬁed and will discuss three main healed slope topography types – 1) Parallel to sub-parallel ﬁll, 2) updip ﬁll, and 3) broad down dip ﬁll (Fig. 6a, b, 6c and 8b), modiﬁed by MTD relief interacting with these factors. Following the instantaneous em- placement of the MTD, in the extensional and contractional domains of the MTD, with the MF and FF facies respectively, sediments deposited in MTD conﬁnement bounded by multiple topographic highs result in what we propose as parallel to sub-parallel ﬁll (Fig. 8b). Topographic inhibitions created by megaclast-bearing MTDs can focus depositional fairways and routing pathways between unburied tops of the megaclasts (Armitage et al., 2009; Georgiopoulou and Cartwright, 2013; Ward et al., 2018; Bull et al., 2020; Nwoko et al., 2020). Therefore, when the deposition is bound by the updip slope of one of the MTD topographic highs of the MF facies, such that the resulting strata onlaps or downlaps the underlying MTD topography, this we have termed ‘updip ﬁll’ (Fig. 6b). Likewise, conﬁnement generated by thrust systems and compression shears can aﬀect ponding proﬁles, with each ponding

of the megaclasts (Armitage et al., 2009; Georgiopoulou and Cartwright, 2013; Ward et al., 2018; Bull et al., 2020; Nwoko et al., 2020). Therefore, when the deposition is bound by the updip slope of one of the MTD topographic highs of the MF facies, such that the resulting strata onlaps or downlaps the underlying MTD topography, this we have termed ‘updip ﬁll’ (Fig. 6b). Likewise, conﬁnement generated by thrust systems and compression shears can aﬀect ponding proﬁles, with each ponding style showing variable degrees of sediment connectivity; thus, in the toe regions of MTDs, sediments preferentially ﬁlls and over spills above such conﬁnement, creating a concave-convex proﬁle (Kenyon and Turcotte, 1985; Schlager and Camber, 1986; Kenter, 1990; Lee et al., 2004; Ferry et al., 2005; Dykstra et al., 2011; Kneller et al., 2016). The updip pinch-out scenario, also observed for the FF facies, accounts for a syn-depositional ﬁll with similar concave-convex pro- ﬁles, focusing transport of subsequent sediment downslope towards the base of the basin (Fig. 6b, d). This mechanism continues post-de- formation, allowing for further deposition of sediments following the gravity ﬂow routing pathways created by the moving mass (Fig. 6b) Therefore, the structurally complicated type I topography of the MF facies contains less connected overburden distribution than FF facies with the same topography type. The degrees of sediment connectivity are represented by the highly serrated signature of ponding for the MF, as opposed to the serrated proﬁle for the FF facies (Fig. 5d, e and 5f). Although both distributions are aﬀected by ﬂow changes controlled by MTD high-relief, the variation in ponding proﬁles are a result of the preferential ﬁll-and-spill on the ‘micro-basins’. This type of inﬁlling occurs where there is a large se- paration distance between topographic highs, in the order of hundreds of meters (MTD type II topography). Broad down-dip ﬁlls are pre- dominant in translational regions of MTDs and hold signiﬁcant sedi- ment volumes (Prather et al., 1998; Kneller, 2003). In places where depositional rates exceed the rate of accommodation space formation, these sediment ponds become connected by aggrading sediments, ﬁlled to the local position of the equilibrium proﬁle (Fig. 6c). Therefore, overburden sediment connectivity in this region is consistent with the low-relief serrated proﬁle peculiar to laterally connected sediment distribution patterns (Kneller et al., 2016; Bull et al., 2020) and is shown for the HF facies with smooth upper surface topography (Fig. 5c, d, 5e and 6a). 6. Conclusions In this study, we have demonstrated that post-MTD sedimentation continue to be preferentially captured by ﬁll-and-spill on top of the rugose MTD top surface in the proximal and distal regions of the survey, resulting in the localised updip, parallel and sub-parallel ﬁll scenarios discussed. The principal novelty and added value of this research is the relationship between seismic facies and their distribution to accom- modation space in slope settings characterized by MTDs. In particular, turbidite sandstones deposited in accommodation space created by MTD high-relief depositional (type I) topography potentially leads to poor sediment connectivity and in proximal and distal domains, may Fig. 8.
